reassignments.rationale: The abstract describes an interrupted time series with collaborative wards as the intervention group and non-collaborative wards as controls.
Rates in flooded areas were compared over time against nonflooded areas with baseline and seasonal adjustment, making this a controlled ITS.
The study estimates influenza vaccine effectiveness by comparing influenza-positive cases with influenza-negative controls under the test-negative design.
Monthly carbapenem use before and after the program was compared with vancomycin as a control series.
The study evaluates sensitivity and specificity of CSF beta-D-glucan testing against confirmed and negative-control specimens.
This paper compares vaccine-effectiveness estimates across alternative influenza-negative control groups within a test-negative case-control study.
Healthcare workers with influenza-like illness were analyzed as influenza-positive cases versus influenza-negative controls to estimate vaccine effectiveness.
Influenza-positive pneumonia patients were compared with influenza-negative pneumonia controls to estimate vaccine effectiveness.
Hospitals implementing chlorhexidine bathing were analyzed over time against hospitals that did not implement bathing.
The paper evaluates use of rotavirus test-negative controls as an alternative control strategy for vaccine-effectiveness case-control studies.
Hospital-onset bacteremia trends before and after the MRSA initiative were contrasted with community-acquired bacteremia as a nonequivalent control.
This is a systematic review and meta-analysis pooling vaccine-effectiveness estimates across alternative control-group choices.
The abstract explicitly reports a test-negative case-control study estimating influenza vaccine effectiveness in children.
The main analysis studies waning with influenza as the outcome and RSV as a negative-control outcome to probe bias.
This is an overview paper discussing when and how quasi-experimental vaccine-evaluation designs are used.
The paper harmonizes an observational study to a randomized trial protocol to make causal effect estimates comparable, which is target-trial emulation logic.
The study jointly uses state-level synthetic control and county-level difference-in-differences, so the mixed policy-evaluation design is best captured by the quasi-experimental umbrella concept.
The paper uses simulated outbreaks to compare power and statistical properties of stepped-wedge and other trial designs.
Outbreak units were compared over time with non-outbreak control units in a controlled interrupted time series.
This is a methods paper proposing a real-time modification of the test-negative design for vaccine-effectiveness studies.
The proposed self-matched method relies on negative-control endpoints to identify naturally acquired immunity while controlling unmeasured heterogeneity.
The policy effects were estimated with multiple interrupted time-series analyses built around synthetic control units.
Population hospitalization trends after MMR revaccination introduction were analyzed with explicit control series.
The core counterfactual for pneumonia mortality after PCV introduction was a composite synthetic control built from other causes of death.
The paper introduces and studies the epidemicity index within spatially explicit mechanistic epidemic models.
This is a guidance paper reviewing policy-impact evaluation designs and their common design problems in COVID-19 studies.
The paper studies recently vaccinated individuals as a negative-control exposure for bias detection in test-negative vaccine-effectiveness studies.
Hospitalized influenza-positive children were compared with influenza-negative controls in a prospective test-negative vaccine-effectiveness study.
Cancer-operation counts were analyzed across COVID periods with intervention and control hospitals defined by COVID-treatment provision.
The study combines a synthetic-control comparison with an event-study border analysis, so a mixed quasi-experimental policy-evaluation label is the best single fit.
The abstract explicitly describes matched retrospective target-trial cohort studies comparing vaccinated and unvaccinated cohorts.
Although additional time-series models were fitted, the comparative identification strategy is a difference-in-differences analysis across UK countries.
The study is explicitly a statewide test-negative case-control analysis of COVID-19 vaccine effectiveness.
This retrospective cohort compares infected individuals with test-negative controls and estimates postacute thrombotic risks using survival-type causal analysis.
The paper evaluates vaccine effectiveness against Omicron infection using a test-negative control study.
Animals with gram-negative mastitis were randomly assigned to treatment arms or no treatment, making this a randomized trial.
Hospitalized influenza-positive patients were compared with influenza-negative, SARS-CoV-2-negative controls to estimate vaccine effectiveness.
The main inferential model is a longitudinal Cox time-to-event analysis of vaccine uptake timing and acceptance determinants.
This is a methods overview on difference-in-differences and synthetic control approaches with illustrative vaccine case studies.
The title identifies this record as a correction notice rather than a substantive quasi-experimental study.
The main design is a self-controlled risk-interval analysis comparing pre-vaccination and post-vaccination windows within cases.
The study evaluates robustness of a self-controlled risk-interval vaccine-safety design; the negative-control outcome is an auxiliary bias check.
The title identifies this record as a correction notice rather than a substantive interrupted time-series analysis.
This is a case report about assay control-line interference causing a diagnostic test failure.
The abstract explicitly reports a test-negative design analysis of RSV vaccine effectiveness.
This is a retrospective cohort comparing positive versus test-negative individuals and following later infection outcomes over time.
The paper centers on validating the typhoid vaccine test-negative design against trial and cohort estimates, with negative controls used as bias indicators.
The study uses mixed-effects logistic regression to assess associations between fecal contamination markers and pathogen detection.
This methodological paper compares instrumental-variable, confounder-adjusted, and difference-in-differences approaches, adds simulation, and argues for triangulated design comparison.
Despite the title wording, the abstract describes a longitudinal cohort with Cox proportional hazards models for incident hypertension after infection.
Expected versus observed infant outcomes were analyzed over time with older children serving as a comparison series.
The primary design is an emulated target trial comparing vaccinated versus unvaccinated post-advanced AKI patients; the ITS is secondary.
The paper uses a balanced interrupted time series with a control group to compare treated and untreated PWID over time.
The study uses registry data to emulate earlier-versus-usual catheter removal strategies under a target-trial framework.

concerns: PMID:38665063 and PMID:39320460 were correction notices with no abstract text in the conflict input, so adjudication relied on the notice title alone.

Several records combined more than one allowed design in the same study (notably PMID:31869328, PMID:33392142, PMID:36177866, and PMID:41069648), so the adjudication necessarily collapsed each mixed design to a single best-fit allowed concept label.
